Command-line interface for running agents, managing agencies, and controlling swarms

import { Aside, Tabs, TabItem, Steps } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

The animaOS CLI (`animaos`) is the operator entrypoint for animaOS. It handles two different jobs:

- local workspace generation through `create`
- daemon-backed execution through `launch`, `run`, `chat`, and `agents`

`launch` is the primary day-to-day workflow. It turns an agency workspace into a live daemon-backed session and can render the Ink TUI. `run` is the quick plain-text shortcut for one-off work. `chat` gives you a single interactive agent. `agents` inspects what the daemon is currently holding.

## Mental Model

Use the command that matches the job:

- `animaos create` builds a local agent or agency workspace. It does not require the daemon.
- `animaos launch` runs an existing `anima.yaml` workspace against the daemon. This is the main TUI flow.
- `animaos run` is the fastest way to send one task to a temporary agent or swarm.
- `animaos chat` opens an interactive single-agent REPL backed by the daemon.
- `animaos agents` lets you inspect daemon-backed agents already registered in the host.

## Fast Path

<Steps>

1. Start the daemon.

   ```bash
   bun run daemon
   ```

2. Generate an agency workspace.

   ```bash
   animaos create agency content-team \
     -d "Research topics and write high-quality articles" \
     -s 5 \
     --seed
   ```

3. Launch that workspace in the TUI.

   ```bash
   cd content-team
   animaos launch
   ```

</Steps>

## Command Guide

### `animaos create`

Create a single agent workspace or a full agency directory. This is a local filesystem workflow, not a daemon workflow.

```bash
# Interactive mode: choose agent or agency
animaos create

# Single-agent workspace
animaos create agent medicine-helper \
  -d "Research medical literature and summarize risks clearly"

# Full agency workspace
animaos create agency content-team \
  -d "Research topics and write high-quality articles" \
  -s 5 \
  --seed

# Legacy shorthand still creates an agency
animaos create content-team --yes
```

`create` uses configured remote provider credentials when they are available and falls back to a deterministic local scaffolder when no provider key is present.

| Flag | Default | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `--kind` | — | `agent` or `agency`; equivalent to `animaos create agent ...` |
| `-p, --provider` | `auto` | Generation provider. Supports `deterministic`, `openai`, `anthropic`, `google`/`gemini`, `ollama`, `groq`, `xai`/`grok`, `openrouter`, `mistral`, `together`, `deepseek`, `fireworks`, `perplexity`, `moonshot`/`kimi` |
| `-m, --model` | `gpt-4o-mini` | Model for generation |
| `--models` | — | Comma-separated model pool for generated agents |
| `-d, --description` | — | What the agent or agency is supposed to do |
| `-s, --size` | `4` | Agency team size (2-10) |
| `--system` | — | System prompt for single-agent mode |
| `--seed` | — | Generate starter memories for each agent |
| `-y, --yes` | — | Accept the generated team without confirmation |

The generated directory is designed for human editing:

```text
content-team/
├── anima.yaml        # Team config used by launch
├── org-chart.mmd     # Mermaid org chart
├── README.md         # Mission, values, and roster
└── agents/
    ├── orchestrator/
    │   ├── profile.md
    │   ├── assets/
    │   └── memory/
    │       └── seed.json
    └── worker-1/
        ├── profile.md
        ├── assets/
        └── memory/
```

### `animaos launch`

Launch an agency workspace from its `anima.yaml`. This is the primary operator workflow and the main entrypoint for the TUI.

```bash
cd content-team

# TUI one-shot task
animaos launch "Write an article about Rust macros"

# TUI interactive session
animaos launch

# Plain-text one-shot task
animaos launch --no-tui "Write an article about Rust macros"

# Plain-text interactive session
animaos launch --no-tui
```

During launch, the CLI can read `agents/<slug>/memory/seed.json` files and post those memories into the daemon before work begins.

| Flag | Default | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `--api-key` | — | API key override for the daemon-backed run |
| `--no-tui` | — | Disable the Ink TUI and use plain text instead |

Plain-text interactive launch supports:

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `exit` | Quit the session |
| `/help` | Show the available commands |
| `/health` | Recheck daemon connectivity |

### `animaos run`

Run a temporary single agent or a temporary swarm for one task. This is the fastest CLI path when you do not want to create a local agency workspace first.

```bash
# Single agent
animaos run "Summarize the Rust book" -m gpt-4o-mini

# Swarm with supervisor strategy
animaos run "Build a landing page" -s supervisor -m gpt-4o
```

<Aside type="caution">
  `run` is plain-text today. The command keeps a `--no-tui` flag for interface consistency, but daemon-backed `run` currently falls back to plain text instead of rendering the TUI.
</Aside>

| Flag | Default | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `-m, --model` | `gpt-4o-mini` | Model to use |
| `-p, --provider` | `openai` | Provider family for the daemon-backed run |
| `-n, --name` | `task-agent` | Agent name for single-agent mode |
| `-s, --strategy` | — | Swarm strategy: `supervisor`, `dynamic`, or `round-robin` |
| `--api-key` | — | API key override |
| `--no-tui` | — | Force plain-text output |

### `animaos chat`

Start a daemon-backed interactive REPL with one agent.

```bash
animaos chat -m claude-3-5-sonnet -p anthropic
```

Type messages at the `you >` prompt. Type `exit` to quit.

### `animaos agents`

Inspect daemon-backed agents that are currently registered in the host.

```bash
# List all agents
animaos agents list

# Show details for a specific agent by name or id
animaos agents show researcher
```

This is useful when you want to check what the daemon currently holds after runs, launches, or host restarts.

### `animaos mod`

Enable, disable, or list mods.

```bash
animaos mod enable my-mod
animaos mod disable my-mod
animaos mod list
```

## Provider Support

The CLI accepts the same main provider family across `create`, `launch`, `run`, and `chat`.

| Provider | Accepted names | Key env vars | Base URL env var | Notes |
|----------|----------------|--------------|------------------|-------|
| OpenAI | `openai` | `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `OPENAI_KEY`, `OPENAI_TOKEN` | `OPENAI_BASE_URL` | Native OpenAI chat completions |
| Anthropic | `anthropic` | `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `ANTHROPIC_KEY`, `ANTHROPIC_TOKEN`, `CLAUDE_API_KEY` | `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` | Native Anthropic Messages API |
| Google Gemini | `google`, `gemini` | `GOOGLE_API_KEY`, `GOOGLE_KEY`, `GOOGLE_AI_KEY`, `GEMINI_API_KEY`, `GOOGLE_GENERATIVE_AI_API_KEY` | `GOOGLE_BASE_URL` | Native Google `generateContent` API |
| Ollama | `ollama` | `OLLAMA_API_KEY` | `OLLAMA_BASE_URL` | Local inference via OpenAI-compatible HTTP shape |
| Groq | `groq` | `GROQ_API_KEY`, `GROQ_KEY`, `GROQ_TOKEN` | `GROQ_BASE_URL` | OpenAI-compatible |
| xAI | `xai`, `grok` | `XAI_API_KEY`, `XAI_KEY`, `GROK_API_KEY` | `XAI_BASE_URL` | OpenAI-compatible |
| OpenRouter | `openrouter` | `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`, `OPENROUTER_KEY`, `OPENROUTER_TOKEN` | `OPENROUTER_BASE_URL` | OpenAI-compatible |
| Mistral | `mistral` | `MISTRAL_API_KEY`, `MISTRAL_KEY`, `MISTRAL_TOKEN` | `MISTRAL_BASE_URL` | OpenAI-compatible |
| Together | `together` | `TOGETHER_API_KEY`, `TOGETHER_KEY`, `TOGETHER_TOKEN` | `TOGETHER_BASE_URL` | OpenAI-compatible |
| DeepSeek | `deepseek` | `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` | `DEEPSEEK_BASE_URL` | OpenAI-compatible |
| Fireworks | `fireworks` | `FIREWORKS_API_KEY` | `FIREWORKS_BASE_URL` | OpenAI-compatible |
| Perplexity | `perplexity` | `PERPLEXITY_API_KEY` | `PERPLEXITY_BASE_URL` | OpenAI-compatible |
| Moonshot | `moonshot`, `kimi` | `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`, `KIMI_API_KEY` | `MOONSHOT_BASE_URL` | OpenAI-compatible Moonshot/Kimi endpoint |

Notes:

- `--api-key` overrides the environment for the current invocation.
- `create` can also run with `deterministic` when you do not want a remote provider.
- Daemon-backed commands forward provider settings into host-backed runs; switching keys does not require a daemon restart.

## Environment

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `ANIMA_DAEMON_URL` | Override the daemon base URL for CLI commands. Defaults to `http://127.0.0.1:8080`. |
| Provider key env vars | Credentials used for generation or daemon-backed execution. See the provider table above. |
| Provider base URL env vars | Override provider endpoints such as `OPENAI_BASE_URL`, `OLLAMA_BASE_URL`, or `OPENROUTER_BASE_URL`. |
| `ANIMAOS_WORKSPACE_ROOT` | Workspace root used by mod configuration and workspace-aware tooling. |

## Good Defaults

- Use `create` when you want a durable human-editable workspace.
- Use `launch` when you want the best operator experience and the TUI.
- Use `run` when you only need a quick one-off answer.
- Use `chat` when you want an agent REPL without creating a workspace first.
- Use `agents` when you are debugging daemon-held state.

Run, chat, inspect, and operate daemon-backed workflows from the CLI

import { Aside } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

These commands talk directly to the daemon instead of generating local workspace files.

## Choose the Right Command

| Command | Use it when | Notes |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| `run` | You want one quick result right now | Creates a temporary agent or swarm and prints plain text |
| `chat` | You want an interactive single-agent REPL | Creates one daemon-backed agent and keeps the conversation open |
| `agents` | You want to inspect daemon-held agents | Useful for debugging or checking host state |
| `mod` | You want to enable, disable, or list mods | Workspace/operator configuration surface |

## `animaos run`

```bash
# Single agent
animaos run "Summarize the Rust book" -m gpt-4o-mini

# Swarm
animaos run "Build a landing page" -s supervisor -m gpt-4o
```

`run` is the fastest daemon-backed CLI path. It is ideal when you do not want to create a workspace first.

| Flag | Default | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `-m, --model` | `gpt-4o-mini` | Model to use |
| `-p, --provider` | `openai` | Provider family |
| `-n, --name` | `task-agent` | Agent name for single-agent mode |
| `-s, --strategy` | — | Swarm strategy: `supervisor`, `dynamic`, `round-robin` |
| `--api-key` | — | API key override |
| `--no-tui` | — | Force plain-text output |

<Aside type="caution">
  `run` is plain-text today. The `--no-tui` flag exists for interface consistency, but daemon-backed `run` does not currently render the Ink TUI.
</Aside>

## `animaos chat`

```bash
animaos chat -m claude-3-5-sonnet -p anthropic
```

`chat` creates a daemon-backed agent and opens a terminal REPL with the `you >` prompt. Type `exit` to quit.

This is the right choice when you want iterative back-and-forth without generating a whole agency workspace.

## `animaos agents`

```bash
# List all registered daemon-backed agents
animaos agents list

# Show one by name or id
animaos agents show researcher
```

Use this when you want to inspect host state after launches, retries, or debugging sessions.

## `animaos mod`

```bash
animaos mod enable my-mod
animaos mod disable my-mod
animaos mod list
```

The mod surface is the operational side of the CLI rather than the runtime side.

## Targeting a Different Daemon

The CLI defaults to `http://127.0.0.1:8080`, but daemon-backed commands can be pointed elsewhere:

```bash
ANIMA_DAEMON_URL=http://127.0.0.1:8081 animaos agents list
```

That is useful for staging, alternate local ports, or multiple hosts.

Create, inspect, and launch agency workspaces from the animaOS CLI

import { Aside, Card, CardGrid } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

This page covers the CLI workflow that starts on disk rather than in the daemon: generate an agent or agency workspace locally, edit it like normal project files, then launch it against the host.

## Core Split

- `animaos create` is local. It writes files and does not require the daemon.
- `animaos launch` is daemon-backed. It reads `anima.yaml`, creates a runtime swarm in the host, and runs tasks through the daemon.

That split is the main reason the CLI is practical for real work: you can shape the workspace locally, review it, and only then execute it.

<CardGrid>
  <Card title="Create">
    Generate the agent or agency workspace locally with `animaos create`.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Refine">
    Edit `anima.yaml`, agent profiles, and optional seed memories before execution.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Launch">
    Run the workspace against the daemon with `animaos launch` and use the TUI or plain text.
  </Card>
</CardGrid>

## Create a Workspace

```bash
# Interactive picker
animaos create

# Single-agent workspace
animaos create agent medicine-helper \
  -d "Research medical literature and summarize risks clearly"

# Full agency workspace
animaos create agency content-team \
  -d "Research topics and write high-quality articles" \
  -s 5 \
  --seed
```

`create` uses configured remote provider credentials when available and falls back to a deterministic local scaffolder when no provider key is present.

| Flag | Default | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `--kind` | — | `agent` or `agency` |
| `-p, --provider` | `auto` | Generation provider. Supports `deterministic` and the daemon-facing provider family |
| `-m, --model` | `gpt-4o-mini` | Model for generation |
| `--models` | — | Comma-separated model pool for generated agents |
| `-d, --description` | — | What the agent or agency should do |
| `-s, --size` | `4` | Agency team size |
| `--system` | — | System prompt for single-agent mode |
| `--seed` | — | Generate starter memories for each agent |
| `-y, --yes` | — | Skip confirmation |

## Workspace Shape

The generated workspace is designed to be edited by humans before launch:

```text
content-team/
├── anima.yaml
├── org-chart.mmd
├── README.md
└── agents/
    ├── orchestrator/
    │   ├── profile.md
    │   ├── assets/
    │   └── memory/
    │       └── seed.json
    └── worker-1/
        ├── profile.md
        ├── assets/
        └── memory/
```

The important files are:

- `anima.yaml` for runtime config
- `agents/*/profile.md` for human-readable role shaping
- `agents/*/memory/seed.json` for optional preloaded memories posted at launch time

## Launch a Workspace

```bash
cd content-team

# TUI session
animaos launch

# One-shot task in the TUI workflow
animaos launch "Write a launch announcement"

# Plain-text mode
animaos launch --no-tui "Write a launch announcement"
```

`launch` is the best operator flow when you want the TUI, saved run history, and daemon-backed workspace execution.

| Flag | Default | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `--api-key` | — | API key override for the current daemon-backed run |
| `--no-tui` | — | Disable Ink and use plain text |

<Aside type="tip">
  Use `launch` when you care about durable workspace structure, team composition, saved history, or the TUI. Use `run` only when you want a quick one-off result without creating a workspace first.
</Aside>

## Seed Memories

If the workspace contains `agents/<slug>/memory/seed.json`, the CLI can post those memories into the daemon before the agency starts running. That gives the launched team a useful local memory baseline without hardcoding those facts into the host.

This keeps agency memory bootstrap close to the workspace itself instead of hiding it in global daemon state.

What is animaOS, how it works, and what you can build with it.

import { Card, CardGrid, Aside } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

**animaOS** is a host-agnostic agent runtime designed for building, deploying, and operating autonomous AI agents at scale. It separates the core intelligence engine from host-specific concerns like HTTP, databases, and filesystems — so your agent logic stays pure, testable, and portable across any environment.

At its heart, animaOS is three things:

1. **A runtime engine** that manages agent lifecycles, executes tools, handles LLM provider calls, and maintains state.
2. **A memory system** with hybrid recall — combining BM25 lexical search, vector semantic search, temporal reasoning, and relationship graphs.
3. **A swarm coordinator** that orchestrates multi-agent collaboration with configurable strategies.

Whether you're building a single research assistant or a fleet of cooperating agents, animaOS gives you the primitives to do it without vendor lock-in.

## Philosophy

<CardGrid>
  <Card title="Host-Agnostic Core" icon="seti:rust">
    The Rust core knows nothing about HTTP, databases, or filesystems. It operates on pure data structures and snapshots. Hosts provide the "reality" — the core provides the behavior.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Composable by Design" icon="seti:typescript">
    Agents are configured, not coded. Tools, plugins, providers, and evaluators snap together like LEGO. Reuse components across projects without rewriting boilerplate.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Memory-First" icon="seti:db">
    Agents remember. The memory system isn't an afterthought — it's a first-class citizen with search, recall, evaluation, retention, and relationship tracking built in.
  </Card>
</CardGrid>

## Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      Your Application                       │
│              (SDK, CLI, or custom integration)              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                        Host Layer                           │
│  ┌─────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────┐ │
│  │ Rust Daemon │  │ Elixir Host  │  │  Python Service     │ │
│  │ (ready)     │  │ (planned)    │  │  (planned)          │ │
│  └─────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └─────────────────────┘ │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                      Core Runtime                           │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌───────────────────────┐  │
│  │anima-core│  │ anima-memory │  │   anima-swarm         │  │
│  │- agent   │  │ - BM25       │  │   - coordinator       │  │
│  │  runtime │  │ - vectors    │  │   - message bus       │  │
│  │- events  │  │ - temporal   │  │   - strategies        │  │
│  │- tools   │  │ - relations  │  │                       │  │
│  └──────────┘  └──────────────┘  └───────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

The **core** is where all the intelligence lives. It has zero I/O dependencies — no HTTP framework, no database driver, no filesystem access. This makes it trivial to test, embed in other applications, or port to new environments.

The **host** wraps the core and exposes it over a runtime boundary. The production-ready Rust host provides an Axum HTTP/SSE server. Other hosts (Elixir, Python) are planned for future releases.

<Aside type="note">
  With Postgres-backed daemon persistence enabled, tool steps are written durably and can be reused on an explicit retry. Reuse is request-keyed, not prompt-keyed: callers must repeat the same `retryKey` or `idempotencyKey` in request metadata for a retried run to reuse completed tool results.
</Aside>

## Key Concepts

### Agents

An agent is an autonomous LLM-powered runtime with a configuration, state, and optional tools. You define what it knows, how it behaves, and what it can do — the runtime handles the rest.

- **Configuration** — name, model, provider, system prompt, personality traits
- **Tools** — typed actions the agent can invoke (web search, file I/O, memory access)
- **Plugins** — reusable bundles of tools, providers, and evaluators
- **State** — runtime status, message history, token usage

[Learn more about Agents →](/sdk/agents/)

### Swarms

A swarm is a coordinated group of agents that collaborate on a shared task. The swarm runtime manages turn-taking, message passing, and result synthesis.

- **Strategies** — `round-robin`, `supervisor`, or `dynamic` delegation
- **Manager** — the lead agent that orchestrates the group
- **Workers** — agents that execute subtasks and report back
- **Events** — real-time SSE stream of everything happening inside the swarm

[Learn more about Swarms →](/sdk/swarms/)

### Memories

The memory system gives agents long-term recall through a hybrid retrieval pipeline.

- **Search** — BM25 + vector similarity for finding relevant memories
- **Recall** — fuses lexical, vector, relationship, temporal, recency, and importance signals
- **Evaluation** — automatic deduplication and quality scoring before storage
- **Retention** — configurable decay and cleanup to prevent unbounded growth
- **Relationships** — graph connections between agents, users, and entities

[Learn more about Memories →](/sdk/memories/)

## Getting Started in 60 Seconds

```bash
# 1. Start the daemon
bun run daemon

# 2. Set your provider key
export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."

# 3. Create and run an agent
npx -y @animaOS-SWARM/sdk-cli agent create --name helper --model gpt-4o-mini
```

Or use the TypeScript SDK for full programmatic control:

```typescript
import { agent, createDaemonClient } from '@animaOS-SWARM/sdk';

const client = createDaemonClient();

const helper = await client.agents.create(
  agent({
    name: 'helper',
    provider: 'openai',
    model: 'gpt-4o-mini',
    system: 'You are a helpful assistant.',
  })
);

const result = await client.agents.run(helper.state.id, {
  text: 'What is the capital of France?',
});

console.log(result.result.data?.text);
```

Typed actions, plugin bundles, and agent capability composition in the SDK

import { Aside, Card, CardGrid } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

Actions and plugins are the main composition surface for agent capabilities in the SDK.

<CardGrid>
  <Card title="Action">
    Use `action()` when you want one focused tool with a typed input and output contract.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Plugin">
    Use `plugin()` when the capability should travel as a reusable bundle.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Registration">
    Attach tools and plugins to the agent config before you register the agent with the daemon.
  </Card>
</CardGrid>

## Actions

An action is a typed tool the model can invoke.

```typescript
import { action } from '@animaOS-SWARM/sdk';

const memorySearch = action({
  name: 'memory_search',
  description: 'Search stored memories by keyword',
  parametersSchema: {
    query: { type: 'string', description: 'Search query' },
    limit: { type: 'number', description: 'Maximum matches to return' },
  },
  handler: async (_runtime, _message, args) => {
    const query = typeof args.query === 'string' ? args.query : '';
    return {
      status: 'success',
      data: { text: `Search requested for: ${query}` },
      durationMs: 0,
    };
  },
});
```

The key fields are:

- `name`
- `description`
- `parametersSchema`
- `handler`
- optional `validate`
- optional `examples`

## Register Actions on an Agent

```typescript
const researcher = await client.agents.create(
  agent({
    name: 'researcher',
    provider: 'openai',
    model: 'gpt-4o-mini',
    system: 'Use tools when needed.',
    tools: [memorySearch],
  })
);
```

## Plugins

A plugin groups actions, providers, and evaluators into a reusable module.

```typescript
import { plugin } from '@animaOS-SWARM/sdk';

const researchPlugin = plugin({
  name: 'research',
  description: 'Web search and memory tools for research agents',
  actions: [memorySearch],
  providers: [],
  evaluators: [],
});
```

Plugins are the cleaner choice when the capability set should travel as one unit across multiple agents.

## Practical Capability Split

- choose `action()` for one narrow capability owned by one integration
- choose `plugin()` when several agents should share the same capability pack
- keep runtime-heavy behavior in the host instead of pushing orchestration into the SDK caller

## When to Use What

- use a single `action()` when the capability is narrow and local
- use `plugin()` when the capability is a reusable bundle
- use plain config when no custom behavior is needed

<Aside type="tip">
  The SDK gives you typed composition. The actual runtime execution still happens inside the host, not inside the SDK package.
</Aside>

## Where Next

- [Agents Overview](/sdk/agents/) for the broader agent lifecycle.
- [Lifecycle & Retry](/sdk/agents/lifecycle/) for the run loop and retry-key behavior.

Search, recall, and manage agent memories with the animaOS SDK

import { Aside, Tabs, TabItem } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

The memory system in animaOS provides **hybrid recall** combining BM25 lexical search, vector semantic search, temporal reasoning, and relationship graph traversal. Memories can belong to agents, rooms, worlds, or sessions, and support scoped visibility.

## In This Section

- [Search & Recall](/sdk/memories/recall/) covers lexical search, fused recall, result scoring, and recent-memory reads.
- [Evaluation & Retention](/sdk/memories/operations/) covers evaluated writes, evidence tracing, retention, readiness, entities, and relationships.

## Create a Memory

```typescript
const memory = await client.memories.create({
  agentId: 'agent-uuid-here',
  agentName: 'researcher',
  type: 'fact',
  content: 'Rust daemon memory endpoint created',
  importance: 0.8,
  tags: ['sdk', 'memory'],
  scope: 'shared',
  roomId: 'room-1',
  worldId: 'world-default',
});
```

### `CreateMemoryInput` fields

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|-------|------|----------|-------------|
| `agentId` | `string` | ✅ | Owning agent identifier |
| `agentName` | `string` | ✅ | Agent name at creation time |
| `type` | `MemoryType` | ✅ | Memory category |
| `content` | `string` | ✅ | Memory text |
| `importance` | `number` | ✅ | Importance score (0–1) |
| `tags` | `string[] \| null` | — | Optional searchable tags |
| `scope` | `MemoryScope` | — | Visibility: `shared`, `private`, or `room` |
| `roomId` | `string` | — | Room context |
| `worldId` | `string` | — | World context |
| `sessionId` | `string` | — | Session context |

### `MemoryType` values

Common types include `fact`, `reflection`, `conversation`, `goal`, `plan`, `observation`, and `event`. The type is stored as a string and can be extended for your domain.

### `MemoryScope` values

| Value | Behavior |
|-------|----------|
| `shared` | Visible to all agents in the same context |
| `private` | Visible only to the owning agent |
| `room` | Visible to agents in the specified room |

## Search Memories

Search uses a hybrid of lexical (BM25) and vector similarity scoring.

```typescript
const results = await client.memories.search('daemon memory', {
  agentName: 'researcher',
  type: 'fact',
  limit: 5,
  minImportance: 0.5,
});

for (const result of results) {
  console.log(`[${result.score.toFixed(3)}] ${result.memory.content}`);
}
```

### `MemorySearchOptions`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `agentId` | `string` | Filter by owning agent |
| `agentName` | `string` | Filter by agent name |
| `type` | `MemoryType` | Filter by memory type |
| `scope` | `MemoryScope` | Filter by visibility scope |
| `roomId` | `string` | Filter by room |
| `worldId` | `string` | Filter by world |
| `sessionId` | `string` | Filter by session |
| `limit` | `number` | Maximum results |
| `minImportance` | `number` | Minimum importance threshold |

### `MemorySearchResult`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `memory` | `Memory` | The matched memory |
| `score` | `number` | Combined relevance score |

## Recent Memories

Retrieve the most recently created memories with optional filtering:

```typescript
const memories = await client.memories.recent({
  agentId: 'agent-uuid-here',
  scope: 'shared',
  limit: 20,
});
```

### `RecentMemoriesOptions`

Same fields as `MemorySearchOptions` except `type` and `minImportance`.

## Recall

**Recall** is the advanced retrieval API. It fuses multiple signals into a single ranked result set:

- **Lexical** — BM25 keyword matching
- **Vector** — Semantic embedding similarity
- **Relationship** — Graph proximity via `AgentRelationship`
- **Temporal** — Time-decayed relevance
- **Recency** — How recently the memory was created
- **Importance** — The stored importance score

```typescript
const results = await client.memories.recall('evidence probe', {
  entityId: 'user-1',
  agentId: 'agent-1',
  temporalLimit: 7,
  limit: 3,
});

for (const r of results) {
  console.log(`score ${r.score.toFixed(3)} — ${r.memory.content}`);
  console.log(`  lexical: ${r.lexicalScore.toFixed(3)}`);
  console.log(`  vector: ${r.vectorScore.toFixed(3)}`);
  console.log(`  relationship: ${r.relationshipScore.toFixed(3)}`);
}
```

### `MemoryRecallOptions`

All `MemorySearchOptions` plus:

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `entityId` | `string` | Target entity for relationship scoring |
| `recallAgentId` | `string` | Agent performing the recall |
| `lexicalLimit` | `number` | Max lexical candidates |
| `recentLimit` | `number` | Max recent candidates |
| `relationshipLimit` | `number` | Max relationship candidates |
| `temporalLimit` | `number` | Temporal window size |

### `MemoryRecallResult`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `memory` | `Memory` | The recalled memory |
| `score` | `number` | Fused composite score |
| `lexicalScore` | `number` | BM25 contribution |
| `vectorScore` | `number` | Embedding similarity contribution |
| `relationshipScore` | `number` | Graph proximity contribution |
| `temporalScore` | `number` | Temporal relevance contribution |
| `recencyScore` | `number` | Recency contribution |
| `importanceScore` | `number` | Importance contribution |

## Evaluate and Store

Before storing a memory, you can run it through the evaluation pipeline. The evaluator decides whether to **store**, **merge** with an existing memory, or **ignore** the input.

```typescript
const outcome = await client.memories.addEvaluated({
  agentId: 'agent-1',
  agentName: 'researcher',
  type: 'fact',
  content: 'User prefers concise release notes',
  importance: 0.4,
  tags: ['preference'],
  minContentChars: 8,
  minImportance: 0.1,
});

console.log(outcome.evaluation.decision); // 'store' | 'merge' | 'ignore'
console.log(outcome.evaluation.reason);   // Human-readable explanation
console.log(outcome.evaluation.score);    // Confidence score

if (outcome.memory) {
  console.log('Stored memory ID:', outcome.memory.id);
}
```

### `MemoryEvaluationDecision`

| Value | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `store` | Save as a new memory |
| `merge` | Combine with an existing duplicate memory |
| `ignore` | Discard — too low quality or redundant |

### `MemoryEvaluationOutcome`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `evaluation` | `MemoryEvaluation` | Evaluation result |
| `memory` | `Memory \| null` | The stored/merged memory, if any |

### Evaluate without storing

If you want to preview the evaluation decision before committing:

```typescript
const evaluation = await client.memories.evaluate({
  agentId: 'agent-1',
  agentName: 'researcher',
  type: 'fact',
  content: 'Something happened',
  importance: 0.5,
});

console.log(evaluation.decision, evaluation.reason);
```

## Memory Trace

Trace the evidence graph for a specific memory — its relationships and linked entities.

```typescript
const trace = await client.memories.trace('memory-uuid-here');

console.log('Memory:', trace.memory.content);
console.log('Relationships:', trace.relationships.length);
console.log('Entities:', trace.entities.length);
```

### `MemoryEvidenceTrace`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `memory` | `Memory` | The traced memory |
| `relationships` | `AgentRelationship[]` | Connected relationships |
| `entities` | `MemoryEntity[]` | Linked entities |

## Retention

Apply automatic memory cleanup with configurable decay and importance thresholds.

```typescript
const report = await client.memories.applyRetention({
  minImportance: 0.2,
  maxMemories: 1000,
  maxAgeMillis: 30 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000, // 30 days
  decayHalfLifeMillis: 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000, // 1 day
});

console.log('Decayed:', report.decayedMemories.length);
console.log('Removed:', report.removedMemoryIds.length);
console.log('Relationships removed:', report.removedRelationshipIds.length);
```

### `MemoryRetentionInput`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `maxAgeMillis` | `number` | Delete memories older than this |
| `minImportance` | `number` | Delete memories below this importance |
| `maxMemories` | `number` | Keep only the N most important |
| `decayHalfLifeMillis` | `number` | Half-life for exponential importance decay |

### `MemoryRetentionReport`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `decayedMemories` | `MemoryImportanceAdjustment[]` | Memories whose importance was reduced |
| `removedMemoryIds` | `string[]` | Deleted memory IDs |
| `removedRelationshipIds` | `string[]` | Deleted relationship IDs |

## Readiness

Check whether the memory subsystem is fully operational — embeddings loaded, vector store ready, and evaluation pipeline passing self-checks.

```typescript
const ready = await client.memories.readiness();

console.log('Overall:', ready.passed);
console.log('Vector count:', ready.embeddings.vectorCount);
console.log('Embedding provider:', ready.embeddings.provider);
console.log('Model:', ready.embeddings.model);
console.log('Dimension:', ready.embeddings.dimension);
console.log('Evaluation checks:', ready.evaluation.passedChecks, '/', ready.evaluation.totalChecks);
```

### `MemoryReadiness`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `passed` | `boolean` | Overall health status |
| `embeddings` | `MemoryEmbeddingStatus` | Embedding engine status |
| `evaluation` | `MemoryEvalReport` | Self-test results |

### `MemoryEmbeddingStatus`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `enabled` | `boolean` | Whether embeddings are active |
| `provider` | `string` | Embedding provider name |
| `model` | `string` | Model identifier |
| `dimension` | `number` | Vector dimensionality |
| `vectorCount` | `number` | Number of vectors stored |
| `persisted` | `boolean` | Whether vectors are persisted |
| `storageFile` | `string \| null` | Path to vector store file |

## Relationships

Memories are connected via relationships between agents and entities. You can create and query these relationships explicitly.

### Create a relationship

```typescript
const relationship = await client.memories.createRelationship({
  sourceAgentId: 'agent-a',
  sourceKind: 'agent',
  targetAgentId: 'agent-b',
  targetKind: 'agent',
  relationshipType: 'collaborator',
  strength: 0.9,
  confidence: 0.85,
});
```

### Query relationships

```typescript
const relationships = await client.memories.relationships({
  agentId: 'agent-a',
  relationshipType: 'collaborator',
  minStrength: 0.5,
  limit: 10,
});
```

### `AgentRelationshipOptions`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `agentId` | `string` | Filter by participating agent |
| `entityId` | `string` | Filter by entity |
| `sourceKind` | `RelationshipEndpointKind` | Filter by source type |
| `sourceAgentId` | `string` | Filter by source agent |
| `targetKind` | `RelationshipEndpointKind` | Filter by target type |
| `targetAgentId` | `string` | Filter by target agent |
| `relationshipType` | `string` | Filter by relationship type |
| `roomId` | `string` | Filter by room |
| `worldId` | `string` | Filter by world |
| `sessionId` | `string` | Filter by session |
| `minStrength` | `number` | Minimum relationship strength |
| `minConfidence` | `number` | Minimum confidence |
| `limit` | `number` | Maximum results |

## Entities

Entities are typed objects that can participate in the relationship graph.

```typescript
// Create an entity
const entity = await client.memories.createEntity({
  kind: 'user',
  id: 'user-42',
  name: 'Alice',
  aliases: ['al', 'ali'],
  summary: 'A power user who prefers CLI tools.',
});

// List entities
const entities = await client.memories.entities({
  kind: 'user',
  limit: 50,
});
```

### `MemoryEntity`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `kind` | `RelationshipEndpointKind` | Entity type |
| `id` | `string` | Unique identifier |
| `name` | `string` | Display name |
| `aliases` | `string[]` | Alternative names |
| `summary` | `string \| null` | Optional description |
| `createdAt` | `number` | Unix timestamp (ms) |
| `updatedAt` | `number` | Unix timestamp (ms) |

This page covers the write-side and maintenance-side memory APIs.

<CardGrid>
  <Card title="Evaluate">
    Score candidate memory before storing it, merging it, or ignoring it.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Trace">
    Inspect linked entities and relationships around one stored memory.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Retain">
    Apply cleanup and decay rules so the memory graph does not drift into noise.
  </Card>

    Check embeddings, vector counts, and evaluation health before relying on recall.
  </Card>

## Evaluate Before Writing

```typescript
const evaluation = await client.memories.evaluate({
  agentId: 'agent-1',
  agentName: 'researcher',
  type: 'fact',
  content: 'Something happened',
  importance: 0.5,
});

console.log(evaluation.decision, evaluation.reason);
```

Use this when your app wants to preview the memory decision without storing anything yet.

## Evaluate and Store

```typescript
const outcome = await client.memories.addEvaluated({
  agentId: 'agent-1',
  agentName: 'researcher',
  type: 'fact',
  content: 'User prefers concise release notes',
  importance: 0.4,
  tags: ['preference'],
  minContentChars: 8,
  minImportance: 0.1,
});

console.log(outcome.evaluation.decision, outcome.memory?.id);
```

## Evidence Trace

```typescript
const trace = await client.memories.trace('memory-uuid-here');

console.log(trace.memory.content);
console.log(trace.relationships.length, trace.entities.length);
```

Use this when the app needs to explain why a memory matters and what it connects to.

## Maintenance Loop

The durable pattern is straightforward:

1. evaluate or write candidate memory
2. inspect trace when you need explainability
3. monitor readiness when operating the subsystem
4. run retention to keep long-lived state useful

## Retention and Cleanup

```typescript
const report = await client.memories.applyRetention({
  minImportance: 0.2,
  maxMemories: 1000,
  maxAgeMillis: 30 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000,
  decayHalfLifeMillis: 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000,
});

console.log(report.removedMemoryIds.length);
```

## Readiness

```typescript
const ready = await client.memories.readiness();
console.log(ready.passed, ready.embeddings.vectorCount);
```

This is the operational check for the memory subsystem: embeddings, vector counts, and evaluation self-tests.

## Entities and Relationships

```typescript
const entity = await client.memories.createEntity({
  kind: 'user',
  id: 'user-42',
  name: 'Alice',
});

const relationship = await client.memories.createRelationship({
  sourceAgentId: 'agent-a',
  sourceKind: 'agent',
  targetAgentId: 'agent-b',
  targetKind: 'agent',
  relationshipType: 'collaborator',
  strength: 0.9,
  confidence: 0.85,
});

console.log(entity.id, relationship.relationshipType);
```

These APIs let your app build explicit graph structure instead of inferring everything from plain text alone.

<Aside type="note">
  Retrieval and write-side operations complement each other. `search()` and `recall()` find useful memory. Evaluation, retention, entities, and relationships keep that memory graph healthy over time.
</Aside>

- [API Reference](/sdk/reference/) for full type signatures.
Lexical search, fused recall, recent-memory reads, and score interpretation in the SDK

import { Aside, Card, CardGrid } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

Memory retrieval in animaOS has two main levels:

- `search()` for straightforward lexical or hybrid lookup
- `recall()` for fused ranking across lexical, vector, relationship, temporal, recency, and importance signals

<CardGrid>
  <Card title="Search">
    Use `search()` for direct keyword or filter-driven lookup.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Recall">
    Use `recall()` when you want a fused result ranking instead of just raw matches.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Recent">
    Use `recent()` when freshness matters more than retrieval scoring.
  </Card>
</CardGrid>

## Search

```typescript
const results = await client.memories.search('daemon memory', {
  agentName: 'researcher',
  type: 'fact',
  limit: 5,
  minImportance: 0.5,
});

for (const result of results) {
  console.log(result.score, result.memory.content);
}
```

## Recent Memories

```typescript
const memories = await client.memories.recent({
  agentId: 'agent-uuid-here',
  scope: 'shared',
  limit: 20,
});
```

## Fused Recall

```typescript
const results = await client.memories.recall('evidence probe', {
  entityId: 'user-1',
  agentId: 'agent-1',
  temporalLimit: 7,
  limit: 3,
});

for (const r of results) {
  console.log(r.score, r.memory.content);
  console.log(r.lexicalScore, r.vectorScore, r.relationshipScore);
}
```

## How to Read Recall Scores

Recall combines several signals:

- lexical match
- vector similarity
- relationship proximity
- temporal relevance
- recency
- stored importance

The point is not to memorize the weighting math. The point is to understand that recall is intentionally richer than plain search.

<Aside type="tip">
  Reach for `search()` when the user already knows what they are looking for. Reach for `recall()` when the system should infer the best supporting memories from multiple signals.
</Aside>

- [Memories Overview](/sdk/memories/) for the full memory surface.
- [Evaluation & Retention](/sdk/memories/operations/) for write-side and cleanup operations.
Get up and running with the animaOS SDK in minutes
Complete SDK API reference with types, errors, and helper functions

import { Aside } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

The animaOS SDK is organized around a single `DaemonClient` with three domain-specific sub-clients: `AgentsClient`, `SwarmsClient`, and `MemoriesClient`.

The SDK communicates with any animaOS-compatible host over HTTP and SSE. The [Rust daemon](https://github.com/G9000/animaOS-SWARM/tree/main/hosts/rust-daemon) in this repo is the reference implementation. Hosts that implement the same HTTP contract (agents, swarms, memories, health endpoints) work out of the box.

## `DaemonClient`

```typescript
import { createDaemonClient, DaemonClient } from '@animaOS-SWARM/sdk';

const client = createDaemonClient(options);
```

### Constructor options (`DaemonClientOptions`)

| Field | Type | Default | Description |
|-------|------|---------|-------------|
| `baseUrl` | `string` | `http://127.0.0.1:8080` | Daemon HTTP endpoint |
| `fetch` | `FetchLike` | `globalThis.fetch` | Custom fetch implementation |

### Methods

| Method | Signature | Description |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| `health()` | `() => Promise<DaemonHealth>` | Check daemon status |
| `requestJson<T>(path, init?)` | `(string, RequestInit?) => Promise<T>` | Generic typed JSON request |
| `subscribe<T>(path, init?)` | `(string, RequestInit?) => AsyncGenerator<DaemonEvent<T>>` | SSE event stream |

### `DaemonHealth`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `status` | `string` | Health status: `ok`, `degraded`, or `unavailable` |

### `DaemonEvent<T>`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `event` | `string` | SSE event name |
| `data` | `T` | Parsed JSON payload |

---

## `AgentsClient`

Accessed via `client.agents`.

| Method | Signature | Description |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| `create` | `(config: AgentConfig) => Promise<AgentSnapshot>` | Create a new agent |
| `list` | `() => Promise<AgentSnapshot[]>` | List all agents |
| `get` | `(agentId: string) => Promise<AgentSnapshot>` | Get agent by ID |
| `run` | `(agentId: string, input: Content) => Promise<AgentRunResponse>` | Execute a task |
| `recentMemories` | `(agentId: string, options?) => Promise<AgentMemory[]>` | Fetch recent memories |

### `AgentSnapshot`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `state` | `AgentState` | Runtime state |
| `messageCount` | `number` | Total messages processed |
| `eventCount` | `number` | Total events emitted |
| `lastTask` | `TaskResult<Content> \| null` | Result of the most recent task |

### `AgentRunResponse`

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|

## Errors

| Class | Thrown When | Properties |
|-------|-------------|------------|
| `DaemonHttpError` | Daemon returns non-2xx status | `status: number`, `body: unknown` |
| `DaemonConnectionError` | Network failure reaching daemon | `cause: unknown` |

### Example: structured error handling

```typescript
import { DaemonHttpError, DaemonConnectionError } from '@animaOS-SWARM/sdk';

try {
  const result = await client.agents.run(agentId, { text: 'Hello' });
} catch (error) {
  if (error instanceof DaemonHttpError) {
    console.error(`Daemon error ${error.status}:`, error.body);
  } else if (error instanceof DaemonConnectionError) {
    console.error('Cannot reach daemon:', error.cause);
  } else {
    throw error;
  }
}
```

Terminal UI for visual swarm monitoring and agent interaction

import { Aside } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

The animaOS Terminal UI (TUI) is the main operator surface for agency work. It is built with [Ink](https://github.com/vadimdemedes/ink) and runs inside your terminal, giving you a real-time view of swarm execution, tools, results, health, and saved history.

The TUI sits behind `animaos launch`. It is designed for agency workspaces that already have an `anima.yaml`.

<Aside type="caution">
  Use `animaos launch` for the TUI. `animaos run` is plain-text today and does not render the TUI for daemon-backed runs.
</Aside>

## In This Section

- [Commands & States](/tui/commands/) covers slash commands, health checks, keyboard flow, and daemon-down behavior.
- [History & Resume](/tui/history/) covers `anima-history.json`, saved runs, retry flows, and resumed-session inspection.

## Recommended Flow

1. Start the daemon.

   ```bash
   bun run daemon
   ```

2. Create an agency workspace if you do not already have one.

   ```bash
   animaos create agency content-team --seed
   ```

3. Launch the workspace in the TUI.

   ```bash
   cd content-team
   animaos launch
   ```

## Launch Modes

```bash
# Launch an agency in the TUI
cd content-team
animaos launch

# One-shot task inside the TUI session
animaos launch "Build a website"

# Plain-text fallback for automation or CI
animaos launch --no-tui "Build a website"
```

`launch` uses the workspace config in `anima.yaml`, can seed daemon memories from `agents/<slug>/memory/seed.json`, and keeps local launch history for later browsing and replay.

## What the TUI Is Good At

- watching a swarm work in real time
- tracking which agent is active and what tools are being called
- checking whether the daemon is healthy without leaving the terminal
- browsing recent run history and replaying prior work
- staying productive when the daemon temporarily drops by switching into command-only mode

Use the TUI subsections in the sidebar if you want the operator details instead of the overview.


## Layout

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  animaOS  │  content-team  │  supervisor   │
├───────────┴────────────────┴───────────────┤
│  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ Agents  │  │ Message Stream          │  │
│  │ ★ Alice │  │ Alice > Researching...  │  │
│  │ • Bob   │  │ Bob > Drafting...       │  │
│  │ • Carol │  │                         │  │
│  └─────────┘  └─────────────────────────┘  │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │ Result Log                             │ │
│  │ • Task completed — 12.3s               │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  task > _                                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Status: running  │  Tokens: 4,231          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Panels

| Panel | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **Agents** | Shows the orchestrator and workers, plus their current status |
| **Message Stream** | Real-time swarm messages and lifecycle events |
| **Tool / Trace Views** | Shows tool calls, arguments, and returned results |
| **Result Log** | Stores completed tasks with duration and token summaries |
| **History View** | Lets you browse saved runs and resume inspection |
| **Input Bar** | Accepts tasks and slash commands |
| **Status Bar** | Shows run status, health state, and daemon-related hints |

## Operator States

The TUI is not just a pretty event stream. It has explicit operator behavior for the hard parts of local runtime work:

- **Preflight health checks** warn you when the daemon is down before a task is sent.
- **Command-only mode** appears when the daemon is unavailable, blocking task submission while still allowing health checks and help.
- **Recovery messaging** tells you when daemon connectivity returns.
- **History and resume flows** let you reopen saved runs and retry them without rebuilding context from scratch.

## Slash Commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `/help` | Show available commands |
| `/health` | Check daemon connectivity and report recovery or failure state |
| `/history` | Browse saved run history |
| `/retry` | Rerun the most recent task or saved run when retry is available |
| `/clear` | Clear the current result/history surface |
| `/back` | Return from history or resumed-run views |

## Keyboard Shortcuts

| Key | Action |
|-----|--------|
| `Enter` | Submit task |
| `Tab` | Cycle between panels |
| `Ctrl+C` | Quit |

## History and Resume

Launch sessions keep local history so the TUI can show prior runs, inspect them, and offer rerun affordances. In agency workspaces, that history is stored in `anima-history.json` next to `anima.yaml`.

This makes the TUI useful even outside the active run itself:

- browse what happened in prior sessions
- resume inspection after a restart
- retry recent tasks without reconstructing the workspace manually

## Live Telemetry

The TUI subscribes to the daemon's SSE event stream, so you see everything in real time:

- Agent status changes (`idle` → `running` → `completed`)
- Tool invocations with arguments and results
- Token usage updates per agent
- Swarm completion events

That live stream is what makes the TUI the best surface for understanding multi-agent behavior as it happens instead of after the fact.

<Aside type="tip">
  The TUI works best in a terminal that supports true color and at least 80x24 characters. Give it more width if you want the agent list, tool trace, and result history to stay readable during busy runs.
</Aside>
Slash commands, keyboard flow, and operator states in the animaOS TUI

import { Aside, Card, CardGrid } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

The TUI is designed as an operator surface, not just a live log window. That means its commands and state transitions matter.

<CardGrid>
  <Card title="Healthy">
    Task submission is enabled and the TUI streams swarm events, tools, and results live.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Daemon Down">
    The session falls back to command-only behavior so `/health` and `/help` still work.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Recovered">
    The TUI reports recovery explicitly so you know task submission can resume.
  </Card>
</CardGrid>


## Slash Commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `/help` | Show the available command surface |
| `/health` | Recheck daemon connectivity and report healthy, recovered, or unavailable state |
| `/history` | Open the saved run history view |
| `/retry` | Retry the most recent task or resumed run when retry is available |
| `/clear` | Clear the current result/history surface |
| `/back` | Return from history or resumed-run views |

## Keyboard Flow

| Key | Action |
|-----|--------|
| `Enter` | Submit the current task or command |
| `Tab` | Cycle between visible panels |
| `Ctrl+C` | Quit the TUI |

## Operator States

### Healthy daemon

Normal task submission is enabled. The TUI streams swarm events, tool traces, token updates, and final results as they arrive.

### Daemon unavailable

The TUI switches into command-only behavior:

- task submission is blocked
- health checks still work
- help remains available
- the UI tells you to use `/health` or `/help`

That makes the terminal session recoverable instead of forcing you to restart everything.

### Recovery

When the daemon comes back, the TUI reports recovery explicitly so you know the host is reachable again.

## Why This Matters

This command surface is what makes the TUI usable during real local development:

- health checks without leaving the session
- graceful behavior during temporary host failure
- retry and history access without reconstructing context manually

<Aside type="tip">
  If you only need one plain-text answer, use `animaos run`. If you want an operator session with recovery, history, and live telemetry, use `animaos launch`.
</Aside>
Saved runs, retry flows, and resumed-session behavior in the animaOS TUI

import { Aside } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';

The TUI keeps local launch history so prior agency runs can be inspected and retried instead of disappearing once the session ends.

## Where History Lives

For agency workspaces launched through `animaos launch`, run history is stored in:

```text
anima-history.json
```

That file lives next to `anima.yaml` in the agency directory.

## What History Gives You

- browse earlier runs with `/history`
- inspect saved output without rerunning the task immediately
- reopen a prior run after restarting the CLI
- retry recent work with `/retry` when retry is available

## Resumed Views

When you reopen a saved or recent run, the TUI can switch into a resumed-session inspection state. From there:

- `/back` returns to the main session surface
- `/retry` reruns the prior task when the daemon is healthy
- `/clear` clears the current surface

## Failure and Recovery Cases

History is especially useful when local runtime work is messy:

- the daemon was temporarily down
- you want to compare current behavior against a prior run
- you want to rerun the last task without rebuilding the workspace context by hand

<Aside type="note">
  History is local CLI session state, not a database-backed host history API. It complements the daemon rather than replacing host-side persistence.
</Aside>

## Suggested Workflow

1. Launch an agency with `animaos launch`.
2. Run a few tasks.
3. Use `/history` to inspect prior runs.
4. Use `/retry` to rerun the last useful task.
5. Use `/back` to return to the live operator view.
